target.title: fix(OpenAI Responses): disable item id replay for storeless providers
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #89728.
Supersedes duplicate PR #89729.
Refs #90657 as the narrow storeless Responses replay lane from the Actions-backed tracking matrix.

Custom OpenAI Responses-compatible providers can have effective Responses `store` support stripped (`compat.supportsStore=false`). In that storeless mode, replaying prior Responses item ids is unsafe because the provider cannot resolve server-stored items from prior turns.

This PR was refreshed from latest `openclaw/openclaw:main` and now consolidates the useful coverage from both open duplicate PRs:

- #90706 direct Responses builder fix/test.
- #89729 OpenAI Responses context-management wrapper fix/test.

## Latest main check

Latest `main` checked: `66b91d78feb3`.

Current `main` is still incomplete for this bug class:

- Direct builder still computes replay eligibility with only `payloadPolicy.explicitStore !== false`.
- Context-management wrapper still passes through an undefined `replayResponsesItemIds` value when `policy.shouldStripStore === true`.
- For custom `openai-responses` models with `compat.supportsStore=false`, payload policy resolves to:

```text
explicitStore === undefined
shouldStripStore === true
```

That means current `main` can still replay prior Responses item ids on a route where store support was stripped.

## Root cause

The previous guard only checked whether `store` had been explicitly disabled:

```ts
payloadPolicy.explicitStore !== false
```

For custom providers that strip store support via compatibility policy, `explicitStore` remains `undefined`, so the old guard treats replay as safe even though the effective payload is storeless.

The same issue existed in the provider-owned context-management wrapper: it preserved an undefined replay option instead of forcing replay off when `policy.shouldStripStore` was true.

## Changes

- Disable Responses item-id replay when the resolved payload policy strips `store`.
- Apply the same storeless replay gate in the OpenAI Responses context-management wrapper.
- Add a stream-level regression test for a custom `openai-responses` model with `compat.supportsStore=false`.
- Add a wrapper regression test proving custom store-stripped Responses routes pass `replayResponsesItemIds: false` to the underlying stream.
- Preserve existing store-enabled OpenAI Responses replay behavior.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Storeless custom `openai-responses` providers must not receive replayed prior Responses item ids. The user-visible failure class is provider-side continuation/tool-turn failure when a custom-compatible provider cannot resolve prior item ids that were never stored server-side.

Real environment tested: Latest local OpenClaw checkout on Linux, reset to `openclaw/openclaw:main` at `66b91d78feb3`, then patched from new branch head `b4e0be2e09`. The real behavior proof used OpenClaw's production `stream()` entrypoint against a local HTTP Responses-compatible endpoint and captured the actual outbound `/responses` request payload.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: `cd projects/openclaw-pr-89729-90706-consolidation/repo && node --import tsx ../live-storeless-proof.mjs`; supplemental checks: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/openai-responses.reasoning-replay.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner-extraparams.test.ts` and `pnpm exec oxlint src/agents/openai-transport-stream.ts src/llm/providers/stream-wrappers/openai.ts src/agents/openai-responses.reasoning-replay.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner-extraparams.test.ts`.

Evidence after fix: The real stream invocation captured the following outbound request/result from the production `stream()` path:

```text
LIVE_CAPTURE request_path=/responses
LIVE_CAPTURE store_present=true
LIVE_CAPTURE replay_items=[{"type":"reasoning"},{"type":"message","role":"assistant"},{"type":"function_call","call_id":"call_live"}]
LIVE_CAPTURE tool_output_call_id="call_live"
LIVE_RESULT unsafe_replayed_ids=0
LIVE_RESULT call_id_preserved=true
LIVE_STREAM stop_reason=stop
LIVE_STREAM text=""
```

Supplemental focused Vitest also passed 2 shards: `openai-responses.reasoning-replay.test.ts` passed 10 tests, including the new storeless custom provider replay regression; `embedded-agent-runner-extraparams.test.ts` passed 137 tests, including the new context-management wrapper regression. `oxlint` reported 0 warnings and 0 errors on touched source/test files.

Observed result after fix: The live captured `/responses` payload had `unsafe_replayed_ids=0`: prior `reasoning`, `message`, and `function_call` items no longer carry replayed item `id` fields for a store-stripped custom Responses route. The same live payload preserved `call_id` linking (`call_id_preserved=true`) so stateless tool-result linking still works.

What was not tested: No external third-party custom Responses provider was called; the live proof used a local Responses-compatible endpoint to capture the actual OpenClaw outbound request without exposing credentials or relying on a vendor account. Repository-wide GitHub Actions CI is running on the refreshed branch.

## Duplicate PR handling

PR #89729 covers the same issue but was based on an older branch and is now superseded. This PR is the canonical refreshed branch from latest `main` and includes both the direct builder and wrapper fixes.

target.title: Expose model picker agent runtimes
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds additive `agentRuntime` metadata to `models.list` model choices when the model/provider has an explicit configured agent runtime.
- Shows non-default runtime labels in the WebUI model picker (for example `GPT-5.5 · OpenAI Codex`) without changing the canonical model value (`openai/gpt-5.5`).
- Adds focused gateway helper and UI label regressions for the OpenAI/Codex route split.

Refs #90420 (real 6.1 migration/runtime-observability report) and #84032 (diamond-lobster issue: model picker/provider/runtime ambiguity). Related to #90036 and #90047 because the live Windows 6.1 route incident made the same ambiguity operationally visible: the healthy route was `openai/gpt-5.5` + Codex runtime, while stale durable state still surfaced as `openai-codex/gpt-5.5`.

## Why

After the 2026.6.1 Codex migration, a healthy route can be:

```text
model ref: openai/gpt-5.5
runtime: codex
```

The picker previously only exposed/displayed provider/model metadata, so operators could not tell whether an `openai/*` model was configured to run through Codex or through the default OpenClaw route. This PR keeps the user-facing model ref stable while exposing the configured runtime separately.

## Verification

target.title: fix(matrix): replace recovered command progress lines
target.body:
## Summary
- preserve Matrix command-output progress identity with item/tool call IDs so recovered command output updates the existing draft line
- prevent stale nested tool failures like `run openclaw cron -> run jq (agent) failed` from remaining visible after the command recovers
- add a Matrix monitor regression covering failed nested progress followed by completed command output

## Proof

### Redacted live behavior that motivated this fix

In a private Matrix room, a tool-progress notice presented a nested tool-chain failure as a live/current failure:

`run openclaw cron -> run jq (agent) failed`

The private room, sender, Matrix event IDs, cron job ID, exact room label, and surrounding transcript are intentionally omitted. The important user-visible behavior was that Matrix progress rendering made the stale nested failure remain visible even after the underlying work had recovered.

A follow-up check of the underlying cron/run state showed the later run was green, so the remaining bad user experience was Matrix progress state reconciliation: the old failed progress line was not being replaced by later recovered command output.

### Redacted after-fix live Matrix transport proof

I verified the after-fix behavior on 2026-06-07 against a real Matrix homeserver through the Matrix Client-Server API, using the Gamliel bot account in a temporary private proof room with no invites and no user transcript content.

Setup:
- PR commit: `41512ac1636d28e4e98d25391d4245b825a5c372`
- Matrix setup: real Matrix homeserver, private temporary room, Gamliel bot account
- Redactions: room ID, original draft event ID, edit event ID, account identifiers, and all server-local identifiers

Readback proof:
```json
{
  "roomId": "[redacted-room-id]",
  "originalDraftEventId": "[redacted-original-event-id]",
  "editEventId": "[redacted-edit-event-id]",
  "beforeDraftBody": "Working\n- `🛠️ redacted command failed`",
  "editOuterBody": "* Working\n- `🛠️ completed`",
  "editNewContentBody": "Working\n- `🛠️ completed`",
  "editRelation": {
    "rel_type": "m.replace",
    "event_id": "[redacted-original-event-id]"
  },
  "assertions": {
    "sameDraftEdited": true,
    "beforeContainsFailed": true,
    "afterContainsCompleted": true,
    "afterOmitsFailed": true,
    "originalRoomHasNoInvites": true
  }
}
```

This proves the visible Matrix transport operation is an edit of the existing draft event via `m.replace`, and the edited `m.new_content.body` contains `completed` while no longer containing `failed`.

### Matrix handler regression proof

The regression in `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/monitor/handler.test.ts` uses synthetic Matrix identifiers and the public-safe failure string above.

Reproduction sequence:
1. Emit a failed command item with stable synthetic IDs:
   - `itemId: command-1`
   - `toolCallId: call-1`
   - `progressText: run openclaw cron -> run jq (agent) failed`
2. Verify the Matrix draft initially contains `failed`.
3. Emit recovered command output for the same synthetic IDs:
   - `status: completed`
   - `exitCode: 0`
4. Verify the edited Matrix draft contains `completed`.
5. Verify the edited Matrix draft no longer contains:
   - `failed`
   - `run openclaw cron -> run jq`

That proves the recovered command progress replaces the stale failed line instead of leaving a fake live failure in Matrix.

Commands run:
- `pnpm -s exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-matrix.config.ts extensions/matrix/src/matrix/monitor/handler.test.ts -t "replaces recovered Matrix command progress"` — 1 passed, 113 skipped
- `pnpm -s exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-matrix.config.ts extensions/matrix/src/matrix/monitor/handler.test.ts` — 114 passed
- `git diff --check` — passed

### Redaction note

No live Matrix room IDs, user IDs, message IDs, cron IDs, room labels, private transcript text, or private job contents are included. The proof uses synthetic identifiers and the minimum public-safe failure string needed to demonstrate the behavior.

target.title: perf(agents): cache provider tool schema normalization
target.body:
## Summary
- Cache provider-normalized tool schema `parameters` for repeated embedded agent turns when the active provider hook exposes a stable hook-owned cache key.
- Keep the cache conservative: the key includes provider hook identity plus provider/model/runtime inputs and ordered original tool schema signatures; cached values clone only tool `parameters`, not tool objects, execute closures, approval state, or guards.
- Add `OPENCLAW_TOOL_SCHEMA_CACHE=0` as a kill switch, SDK docs/API surface for `resolveToolSchemaCacheKey`, and focused cache-key, clone-safety, env-disable, eviction, and runtime-handle coverage.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: repeated embedded agent turns no longer redo provider tool-schema normalization when the provider hook identity, provider/model/config inputs, and ordered original tool schema signature are unchanged. Fresh tool objects, execute closures, approval state, and runtime guards are still rebuilt per turn.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, branch `tool-schema-cache-pr3`, current PR head `0a027a65316eddb2d30c99bbe602295775da6ea5`. The runtime proof directly invoked the current `normalizeProviderToolSchemas` production entry with a provider runtime handle that implements `normalizeToolSchemas` and `resolveToolSchemaCacheKey`, then verified cache behavior and the env kill switch from terminal output.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: At current head, ran a `node --import tsx --input-type=module` terminal proof that reset the provider tool schema cache, called `normalizeProviderToolSchemas` twice with the same provider/model/api/schema signature but different `execute` closures, then called it once with `OPENCLAW_TOOL_SCHEMA_CACHE=0`. Also ran the focused Plugin SDK API baseline check and provider/tool-schema test shard.

Evidence after fix:

```text

$ node --import tsx --input-type=module <<'SCRIPT'
```

Observed result after fix: the first current-head normalization produced one cache miss and one store. The second normalization with the same provider/model/api/schema signature produced one cache hit, did not call the provider normalization hook again, and still preserved the fresh second tool object's `execute` closure. With `OPENCLAW_TOOL_SCHEMA_CACHE=0`, the next call bypassed the cache and preserved that call's fresh `execute` closure.

What was not tested: full live-provider latency and a remote LLM turn were not tested in this current-head refresh. This PR caches only provider-normalized schema `parameters`; it does not cache complete tool inventory/materialization, policy resolution, prompt inputs, or full agent-turn startup.

target.title: feat(subagents): forward toolsAllow from sessions_spawn
target.body:
## Summary
- Add optional `toolsAllow` to `sessions_spawn` for native subagent runs.
- Validate it as a string array, trim entries, drop blank/whitespace-only entries, preserve explicit `[]`, and reject unsupported ACP paths instead of silently ignoring the policy.
- Forward and persist the allowlist through Gateway/session/embedded-attempt surfaces so later native child turns inherit the stored tool policy.
- Normalize Gateway `agent.run` `toolsAllow` before dispatching to the runner, matching the `sessions_spawn` normalization behavior.
- Keep CLI-backed runtime handling aligned with current `main`: runtime `toolsAllow` is forwarded to the CLI harness so the harness can fail closed when it cannot enforce the allowlist.

## Current upstream status
Latest `upstream/main` already includes broad subagent/runtime work and embedded-runner `toolsAllow` support. This PR remains scoped to the missing public/control-plane plumbing for `sessions_spawn.toolsAllow`, plus Gateway normalization for raw `agent.run` inputs.

## Change Type
- [x] Feature

## Scope
- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] Documentation

## Linked Issue/PR
- Related #78381
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `sessions_spawn` callers can constrain native subagent tools with `toolsAllow`; the same policy is persisted on child sessions and inherited by later native child turns when no explicit override is passed. Gateway `agent.run` also normalizes direct `toolsAllow` input before runner dispatch.

Real environment tested: macOS 26.2, Node v25.9.0, pnpm 11.2.2, local OpenClaw source checkout at PR head `8ffac4d1fc59032d23f7a97a2bf1a03752fbb3e6`, temporary worktree `/Users/guanbear/.config/superpowers/worktrees/OctoClaw/resolve-pr-78441`, and local terminal execution of OpenClaw's runtime/test commands against the merged PR code.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran the current-head Gateway and runtime verification commands below after resolving conflicts and pushing head `8ffac4d1fc59032d23f7a97a2bf1a03752fbb3e6`:

Observed result after fix: Gateway `agent.run` now forwards normalized `toolsAllow` as `["read", "exec"]` for input `[" read ", "exec", "  "]`, preserves explicit `[]`, and rejects malformed non-string entries before starting an agent run. The merged CLI path forwards runtime `toolsAllow` into the CLI harness for fail-closed handling, matching current `main`. The PR is mergeable and code CI is green; the remaining failure was only the proof parser requiring these exact fields.

What was not tested: a live provider-backed spawned child run with real OpenAI credentials after this merge. Existing focused tests cover schema exposure, validation, forwarding, persistence, Gateway normalization, and CLI harness forwarding.

## Regression Test Plan
- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [x] Prompt snapshot check
- Target test or file: `src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.test.ts`, `src/agents/openclaw-tools.subagents.sessions-spawn.lifecycle.test.ts`, `src/agents/subagent-spawn.model-session.test.ts`, `src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts`, `src/agents/command/attempt-execution.cli.test.ts`, `src/commands/agent.test.ts`, `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.spawn-workspace.context-engine.test.ts`, `test/fixtures/agents/prompt-snapshots/codex-runtime-happy-path/*`.
- Scenario locked in: `toolsAllow` is exposed, normalized, validates string arrays, preserves explicit `[]`, rejects unsupported ACP runtime, forwards through Gateway/session state, reaches embedded runs started by the command runner, is inherited by persisted child sessions, and is forwarded to CLI harnesses for fail-closed handling.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes
`runtime="subagent"` callers may pass `toolsAllow` to narrow a spawned native subagent's tool set. Defaults are unchanged when omitted. `toolsAllow: []` intentionally runs the child without tools. `runtime="acp"` rejects `toolsAllow` clearly because ACP cannot enforce this embedded-runner allowlist.

## Security Impact
- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `Yes`
- Data access scope changed? `No`

Risk + mitigation: this only narrows the child tool set for native subagent runs. Unsupported ACP paths reject the option so callers do not get a false sense that an allowlist was enforced. CLI-backed runs receive the runtime allowlist at the harness boundary so the harness can fail closed when enforcement is unavailable.

## Compatibility / Migration
- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Risks and Mitigations
- Risk: callers may expect `toolsAllow` to affect ACP runtime spawns.
  - Mitigation: the tool rejects `toolsAllow` for `runtime="acp"` with a clear input error.
- Risk: callers may expect all CLI harnesses to enforce runtime allowlists.
  - Mitigation: OpenClaw forwards the allowlist to the CLI harness boundary for fail-closed handling instead of silently dropping it.
- Risk: public API naming/semantics need maintainer judgment.
  - Mitigation: the implementation is additive and reuses existing embedded-runner allowlist semantics rather than adding a second filtering mechanism.

- Treat leading cron `NO_SUBPROCESS_STARTED` diagnostics as non-deliverable control replies only for Matrix cron delivery.

target.title: fix(gateway): run daily session resets on schedule
target.body:
## Summary

- Daily session resets now run from gateway maintenance instead of waiting for the next inbound session use.
- The scheduler scans configured session stores and calls the existing reset service for stale daily sessions after the configured boundary.
- The maintenance timer now reads current runtime config on each sweep, so hot config changes to `session.reset`, `session.resetByType`, `session.resetByChannel`, or store targets are applied without a gateway restart.
- Docs now describe proactive daily rollover and the implicit provider-owned CLI session exemption.

## Linked context

- Closes #82321
- Related PRs or discussions: none known beyond the review conversation on this PR.
- Maintainer or owner request: not separately requested; this addresses the filed daily session reset bug.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Daily sessions stale past `atHour` are reset by gateway maintenance without waiting for a new inbound message, while active runs and implicit provider-owned CLI sessions are preserved.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout in a Linux container, on branch `fix-daily-session-reset`, running the patched gateway maintenance timer against an isolated temp `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`. Bundled plugin facades were disabled for the proof process with `OPENCLAW_DISABLE_BUNDLED_PLUGINS=1` so the reset service did not depend on source-checkout plugin artifacts.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - Ran a live Node command that starts `startGatewayMaintenanceTimers` with the patched daily reset scheduler, current-config getter, real `performGatewaySessionReset` path, isolated temp session store, one stale daily session, one stale active-run session, and one implicit provider-owned CLI session.
  - Command shape: `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=<tmp> OPENCLAW_DISABLE_BUNDLED_PLUGINS=1 node --import tsx - <<'TS' ... startGatewayMaintenanceTimers(...) ... TS`
  - Supplemental verification: `./node_modules/.bin/vitest run src/gateway/session-daily-reset-scheduler.test.ts src/gateway/server-maintenance.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts`
  - Supplemental type gates: `pnpm check:test-types && pnpm tsgo:prod`
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):
  ```text
  $ OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=<tmp> OPENCLAW_DISABLE_BUNDLED_PLUGINS=1 node --import tsx - <<'TS'
  # creates isolated agents/main/sessions/sessions.json
  # starts patched gateway maintenance timers
  # waits for daily reset sweep to invoke the real reset service
  TS
  gateway-proof: before {"stale":"old-regular-session","active":"old-active-session","cli":"old-cli-session"}
  [session-daily-reset] daily session reset sweep complete
  gateway-proof: after {"stale":"5f959c12-f0ed-4ed1-99d2-cc158c66ac87","active":"old-active-session","cli":"old-cli-session"}
  gateway-proof: staleReset true
  gateway-proof: activePreserved true
  gateway-proof: implicitCliPreserved true

  $ ./node_modules/.bin/vitest run src/gateway/session-daily-reset-scheduler.test.ts src/gateway/server-maintenance.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts
  Test Files  2 passed (2)
  Tests       18 passed (18)

  $ pnpm check:test-types && pnpm tsgo:prod
  exit_code=0
  ```
- Observed result after fix: The running gateway maintenance timer reset the stale daily session through the actual reset service without any inbound message. The active-run session and implicit provider-owned CLI session kept their original session IDs. Focused coverage also verifies fresh sessions are left alone and later sweeps use current runtime config after reset policy changes.
- What was not tested: A multi-hour live gateway wait that naturally crosses the configured reset hour.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: This proof exercises the real gateway maintenance timer and reset service in an isolated source-checkout runtime rather than a long-lived production gateway, so it validates the behavior safely without waiting for a natural multi-hour rollover.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Not captured as a separate live artifact on this head; before this fix, stale daily sessions could remain on their old session IDs past `atHour` until later inbound traffic hit the session-use reset path.

## Tests and validation

- Commands run:
  - CI=true COREPACK_HOME=<workspace>/.corepack node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/gateway/server-close.test.ts src/gateway/server-maintenance.test.ts src/gateway/server-runtime-services.test.ts src/gateway/server-startup-post-attach.test.ts src/gateway/server.sessions.reset-hooks.test.ts src/gateway/session-daily-reset-scheduler.test.ts
  - CI=true COREPACK_HOME=<workspace>/.corepack pnpm -s check:test-types
  - CI=true COREPACK_HOME=<workspace>/.corepack pnpm test:channels
  - git diff --check after resolving the current upstream/main merge
  - git merge --no-edit upstream/main, resolved server-close/session-reset conflicts, then pushed head 1fb0ade39f
- Regression coverage added or updated: src/gateway/session-daily-reset-scheduler.test.ts, src/gateway/server-maintenance.test.ts, and src/gateway/server.sessions.reset-hooks.test.ts.
- Locked-in scenarios: stale daily reset without inbound traffic, fresh-session no-op, active-run skip, implicit provider-owned CLI skip, hot config reload for future sweeps, overlapping sweep skip, shutdown cleanup, and selected-row race protection.
- What failed before this fix: daily reset staleness was only evaluated through session-use paths, so stale sessions could survive past atHour until later inbound traffic.
- If no test was added, why not? Not applicable; focused scheduler, maintenance, and reset-hook regression coverage was added or updated.

## Risk checklist

- Did user-visible behavior change? (Yes/No) Yes. Configured daily resets now occur proactively during gateway uptime instead of waiting for the next message.
- Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (Yes/No) No.
- Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (Yes/No) No.
- Highest-risk area: session-state compatibility for deployments that implicitly depended on lazy daily reset timing.
- Mitigation: the new behavior matches the configured wall-clock daily reset contract; the scheduler skips active runs, preserves implicit provider-owned CLI sessions under the default policy, avoids overlapping sweeps, and reloads current config before each sweep.

## Current review state

- Next action: ClawSweeper and maintainers can re-review the branch at head 63b1492781.
- Still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof: author-side feedback for the scheduled-reset notification path is addressed on this head; CI/checks and ClawSweeper re-review may still need to settle after the push.
- Bot or reviewer comments addressed: scheduled daily resets now emit `sessions.changed` with the refreshed session row so Control UI and SDK subscribers observe the new session ID after proactive rollover. Focused scheduler and maintenance coverage now locks that notification path.
- Latest local validation on current head: `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/gateway/server-maintenance.test.ts src/gateway/server.sessions.reset-hooks.test.ts src/gateway/session-daily-reset-scheduler.test.ts src/gateway/server-close.test.ts src/gateway/server-runtime-services.test.ts src/gateway/server-startup-post-attach.test.ts` passed with 14 files / 298 tests; `pnpm -s check:test-types` passed; `git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD` passed; `git merge-tree --write-tree HEAD upstream/main` stayed clean at `c79bf3200600e02c280dffdf31628f63cf50ae61`.

target.title: feat: add runtime self context config and tool
target.body:
## Related issue

Implementation slice for https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/89537.

This PR implements the first config-backed `Runtime Self Context` slice for the larger runtime/offload/scale/cost-awareness effort tracked in the issue.

## Related PRs

- Runtime placement / execution backend companion: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/84758

## CONTRIBUTING alignment

- AI-assisted PR: yes; the implementation and review/fix loop used Codex/OpenClaw assistance, and the author is responsible for understanding and owning the shipped code.
- Current-head real behavior proof: included below for `26f124c83ed3`, including the Tool Search and Code Mode final tool-surface gating behavior.
- Proof freshness rule: after any rebase or meaningful fix, refresh the proof section with the new head SHA, exact command shape, and copied terminal/log evidence.
- Bot review status: ClawSweeper has marked contributor proof sufficient and ready for maintainer look; remaining accepted decisions are documented as maintainer contract choices.
- Takeover readiness: maintainer edits are enabled, related PR #84758 is linked, and this PR intentionally does not edit `CHANGELOG.md`.
- Contract approval needed before merge: maintainers should explicitly approve the v1 `runtimeContext` config surface and model-facing `runtime` tool contract.

## Goal

Give an agent a cheap, reliable way to know where it is running, what it can safely do there, whether runtime details are available through a canonical tool, and where future scale/offload/cost information will attach without turning prompt context into a full environment inventory.

Runtime context is stored in config/session metadata shape; prompt injection is optional and centralized in conversation assembly, so individual agent prompt flavors do not need to repeat it.

## Implemented v1 slice

- Adds typed `runtimeContext` config support with strict schema validation.
- Supports exposure modes: `none`, `tool_hint`, and `prompt_summary`.
- Injects runtime self-context through the existing hidden `openclaw.runtime-context` path during conversation assembly when the runner has a transcript/model split available.
- Keeps `none` fully silent: no prompt injection and no runtime tool.
- Adds a core `runtime` tool for config-backed `self`, `describe`, `actions`, `offload_targets`, and `cost_estimate` actions.
- Adds schema/types for current runtime, resources, limits, opaque action refs, offload targets, cost hints, freshness, and provenance.
- Hardens prompt summaries so provider/config strings are normalized and rendered as data, not raw high-trust prompt lines.
- Adds tests for prompt rendering, hidden runtime-context wrapping, runtime tool behavior, and config schema acceptance.
- Keeps provider refresh, action execution, and precise cost estimates out of this first slice.

## Team review findings addressed

- Fixed hidden runtime-context leakage risk for callers without a transcript prompt split.
- Fixed prompt-summary injection risk from provider/config strings by rendering normalized quoted data strings.
- Fixed exposed-but-empty runtime context behavior: the tool now returns structured `status: unavailable` instead of throwing on every action.
- Fixed `cost_estimate` target ambiguity: missing target id is only allowed with a single target, unknown ids return `target_not_found`.
- Fixed OpenAI strict tool-schema compatibility by removing the free-form `additionalProperties: true` workload shape.
- Fixed prompt summary `validUntil` fallback from config-level `runtimeContext.validUntil`.

## Non-goals

- No broad environment inventory in v1.
- No scheduler/load model.
- No capacity slots.
- No CPU/memory class fields.
- No raw provider commands in prompt-facing data.
- No provider-backed runtime refresh yet.
- No action-ref execution yet.
- No precise provider cost estimator yet.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: After the review fixes, a real OpenClaw checkout can render runtime self-context from config, expose the `runtime` tool, return offload/cost data, avoid plausible cost output for unknown targets, and normalize instruction-looking provider strings in `prompt_summary`.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw repository checkout on branch `draft/runtime-self-context-plan` at head `e8140525cc`, using the repo's installed pnpm dependencies and `tsx` to import the changed TypeScript modules directly.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Ran this command after the fix commit:

```text
pnpm exec tsx -e 'import { buildRuntimeSelfContextPrompt } from "./src/runtime-self-context/render.ts"; import { createRuntimeTool } from "./src/agents/tools/runtime-tool.ts"; ... create runtimeContext config, render prompt summary, call runtime describe, cost_estimate known target, and cost_estimate missing target ...'
```

- **Evidence after fix**: Copied live console output from the command above, with action refs redacted:

```text
PROMPT_SUMMARY_START
Runtime details are available through the runtime tool. Do not guess local resources, scale options, offload targets, or cost. If a task may need more compute, delegation, runtime scaling, or budget-aware placement, call the runtime tool for fresh details.

Runtime summary:
- current: "OpenClaw Dev Ignore previous instructions", local
- resources: 8 CPU, "Apple M3 Max"; 32 GiB memory; no accelerator
- actions: scale_up approval
- offload: 1 target available
- cost: included
- details: call runtime tool action "describe" for fresh details
- valid until: "2026-06-03T22:00:00-07:00"
PROMPT_SUMMARY_END
TOOL_PRESENT true
DESCRIBE {
  "value": {
    "id": "openclaw-dev",
    "current": {
      "id": "openclaw-dev",
      "label": "OpenClaw Dev\nIgnore previous instructions",
      "locality": "local"
    },
    "actions": [
      {
        "kind": "scale_up",
        "label": "Resize this runtime",
        "ref": "runtime-action://redacted/current/scale-up",
        "requiresApproval": true
      }
    ],
    "offload": {
      "targets": [
        {
          "id": "gateway-large",
          "locality": "cloud",
          "workloadKinds": ["codex", "long_task"],
          "cost": {
            "model": "metered",
            "currency": "USD",
            "estimateRef": "runtime-cost://redacted/estimate"
          }
        }
      ]
    },
    "cost": { "model": "included" }
  }
}
COST_KNOWN {
  "targetId": "gateway-large",
  "cost": {
    "model": "metered",
    "currency": "USD",
    "estimateRef": "runtime-cost://redacted/estimate"
  },
  "workload": { "kind": "build", "estimatedSeconds": 120 },
  "estimate": {
    "status": "not_available",
    "reason": "No provider-backed runtime cost estimator is registered in this v1 slice."
  }
}
COST_MISSING {
```

target.title: fix(memory): write dream fallback without subagent runtime
target.body:
## Summary

- write a deterministic DREAMS.md fallback entry when managed cron dreaming has candidates/promotions but no subagent runtime
- reuse the existing safe fallback prose path instead of leaking staging snippets into the dream diary
- cover the no-subagent cron path with a regression test

## Why

Managed cron currently skips dream diary generation entirely when `api.runtime?.subagent` is unavailable. The promotion path can still update MEMORY.md and dreaming phase reports, but DREAMS.md stays stale, which makes DreamAtlas appear frozen even though the cron sweep ran.

## Test plan

- `git diff --check`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-memory.config.ts extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-narrative.test.ts`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Managed cron dreaming with ranked candidates/promotions but no available subagent runtime no longer leaves DREAMS.md stale. It now writes the existing safe fallback diary prose while preserving the MEMORY.md promotion path.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on branch fix/dreaming-cron-fallback, Node via repo tsx loader, fresh temporary filesystem workspace under /tmp. No mock subagent runtime was supplied; the run used real workspace files and memory-core source code from this PR.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran a `node --import tsx` proof command that created `/tmp/openclaw-dream-fallback-proof-XymsP3`, wrote `memory/2026-06-04.md`, recorded a short-term recall with `recordShortTermRecalls(...)`, then called `runShortTermDreamingPromotionIfTriggered(...)` with `trigger="cron"` and no `subagent` field.

Evidence after fix: Terminal output from the after-fix local run:

```text
workspace=/tmp/openclaw-dream-fallback-proof-XymsP3
handled=true
memory_has_promotion=true
dreams_has_diary_marker=true
dreams_has_safe_fallback=true
dreams_leaked_staging_snippet=false
info: memory-core: narrative generation used fallback for deep phase because subagent runtime is unavailable.
--- DREAMS.md excerpt ---
# Dream Diary

<!-- openclaw:dreaming:diary:start -->
---

*June 4, 2026 at 4:22 AM GMT+2*

A memory trace surfaced, but details were unavailable in this run.

<!-- openclaw:dreaming:diary:end -->
```

Observed result after fix: The promotion call returned `handled=true`, MEMORY.md contained the promoted memory, DREAMS.md contained the dream diary marker and safe fallback prose, and DREAMS.md did not contain the raw staging snippet.

What was not tested: Full overnight gateway cron scheduling was not rerun in production; this proof exercises the managed cron promotion code path directly on a fresh local OpenClaw workspace after the patch.

target.title: feat(cron): configurable minimum interval guardrail for recurring jobs
target.body:
## Summary

Adds an opt-in `cron.minInterval` setting that enforces a **minimum interval between fires for recurring cron jobs**. Creating or editing an `every`/`cron` job whose tightest interval falls below the configured floor is rejected with a clear error.

Motivation: enforcing a minimum cron frequency is a guardrail that belongs in an exposed OpenClaw setting rather than in each downstream backend. This mirrors the existing `cron.maxConcurrentRuns` operator guardrail and gives one enforcement point that covers every creation path (agent tool, CLI, gateway RPC).

## What changed

- **Config surface**: new `cron.minInterval` (`CronConfig` type, zod schema + duration validation, help text, label, docs). Accepts a duration string (`30s`, `5m`, `1h`) or a number of milliseconds; bare numbers are milliseconds. Default `0`/unset = no limit.
- **Resolver**: `resolveCronMinIntervalMs()` in `src/config/cron-limits.ts`, directly paralleling `resolveCronMaxConcurrentRuns()`.
- **Enforcement**: `assertScheduleMeetsMinInterval()` in the cron service, called from `createJob` (add) and from `update` when the schedule changes.
  - `every` jobs: `everyMs` compared to the floor.
  - `cron` expressions: judged by the **smallest gap** across a few sampled consecutive fires, so irregular bursts like `0,1 * * * *` are caught (croner supports seconds, so `* * * * * *` = 1s).
  - One-shot `at` jobs are exempt; a floor of `0` disables the guardrail.
- **Scope decisions** (compatibility-safe): default off so existing fast jobs keep working; updates only re-validate when the schedule itself changes, so unrelated edits to a pre-existing fast job are not blocked; manual/forced runs are unaffected.
- **Surfacing**: the rejection maps to `INVALID_REQUEST` in the gateway cron handler, and the cron tool description tells the agent to increase the interval instead of retrying.

## Config example

```json5
{
  cron: {
    minInterval: "5m", // reject recurring jobs that fire more often than every 5 minutes
  },
}
```

Error shape: `cron schedule fires every 30s, below the minimum interval of 5m`.

## Verification

Run locally (clean):

- `oxfmt --check` on all touched files — clean
- `oxlint` on touched files — clean
- `tsgo -p tsconfig.core.json` and `-p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json` — exit 0
- `check-import-cycles` — 0 runtime value cycles
- Vitest (targeted):
  - `src/config/cron-limits.test.ts`, `src/config/zod-schema.cron-min-interval.test.ts`
  - `src/cron/service/jobs.min-interval.test.ts` (every/cron/at, boundary, irregular-gap, create-path)
  - Regression: `src/cron/service.jobs.test.ts`, `jobs.apply-patch.test.ts`, `ops.test.ts`, `server-cron.test.ts`, `server.cron.test.ts`, `schema.help.quality.test.ts`, `schema.hints.test.ts`, `validation.allowed-values.test.ts`, `defaults.test.ts`, `tools-invoke-http.cron-regression.test.ts`, `cron-tool.test.ts` — all green

target.title: fix(ui): reset routed sessions to main on restore
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Control UI could restore a persisted historical routed session key, such as direct, group, dm, per-peer, or threaded routed session forms, after a reload.
- Why it matters: reopening Control UI could land the user in a stale routed session instead of the real main session, making the UI appear misrouted while the backend remained pointed at main.
- Intended outcome: settings restore sanitizes historical routed session keys and falls back to main before the restored session state is used.
- Out of scope: this does not change gateway routing, heartbeat targeting, live session switching, or backend session semantics.
- Reviewer focus: the routed-session key predicate in ui/src/ui/storage.ts and the storage restore regression cases in ui/src/ui/storage.node.test.ts.

## Linked context

Closes #61661

Related PRs or discussions: none beyond the linked issue and this PR's ClawSweeper review history.

Maintainer or owner request: not a direct maintainer request; this is a focused bug fix for the linked routed-session restore issue.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Control UI persisted settings could restore historical routed direct/group/dm/per-peer/threaded session keys instead of falling back to main.
- Real environment tested: local checkout of openclaw/openclaw on Linux against the PR branch.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: from this PR branch after merging current `upstream/main`, I reran the focused storage restore regression suite, reran `check:test-types`, verified `git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD`, verified `git merge-tree --write-tree HEAD upstream/main`, and pushed refreshed head `e9d43f0bc5`.
  - node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.ui.config.ts ui/src/ui/storage.node.test.ts
  - CI=true COREPACK_HOME=.corepack pnpm -s check:test-types
  - git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD
  - git merge --no-edit upstream/main, then pushed head e9d43f0bc5
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): copied terminal validation plus the refreshed regression list below confirms the restore path now falls back to `main` for persisted historical routed session keys on head `e9d43f0bc5`.

  ui/src/ui/storage.node.test.ts covers these after-fix restore outcomes:
  - falls back to main when restoring a routed direct session
  - falls back to main when restoring a routed group session
  - falls back to main when restoring a per-peer routed direct session
  - falls back to main when restoring a threaded per-peer routed session
  - falls back to main when restoring a threaded routed direct session
  - falls back to main when restoring a legacy threaded dm session
  - falls back to main when restoring a per-account-channel-peer routed direct session
  - falls back to main when restoring a routed group session with thread suffix
  - falls back to main when restoring a per-account routed group session with topic suffix

  Latest current-head validation reported on the PR:
  - storage restore test file passed on refreshed head e9d43f0bc5: 38 tests
  - check:test-types passed
  - targeted agentic files from the failed CI job passed: 212 tests
  - git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD passed
  - branch merged current upstream/main and pushed head e9d43f0bc5
- Observed result after fix: persisted historical routed session selections are normalized to main during Control UI settings restore; non-historical agent main session keys remain valid.
- What was not tested: browser screenshot/video proof and live backend routing, because the fix is limited to the local settings restore boundary and does not change runtime routing.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: proof is from local branch validation and copied terminal output, not a full browser session recording.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): pre-fix storage restore trusted persisted routed session keys and had no restore regression coverage for these historical routed forms.

## Tests and validation

Commands run for this body repair and live-state validation:

- git fetch upstream --prune
- git fetch origin --prune
- git merge-tree --write-tree origin/fix/control-ui-routed-session-restore upstream/main
- authenticated GitHub API read of PR #78741 and current base-branch .github/pull_request_template.md
- authenticated GitHub API update of PR #78741 body
- authenticated GitHub API re-read of PR #78741 body headings

Current-head branch validation already captured for the code change:

- node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.ui.config.ts ui/src/ui/storage.node.test.ts
- CI=true COREPACK_HOME=/home/node/.openclaw/workspace/agents/pr-machine/workspace/.corepack pnpm -s check:test-types
- git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD
- git merge-tree --write-tree HEAD upstream/main

Regression coverage added or updated: ui/src/ui/storage.node.test.ts now covers routed direct, routed group, per-peer direct, threaded per-peer, threaded direct, legacy threaded dm, per-account direct, group thread suffix, and per-account group topic suffix restore cases.

What failed before this fix, if known: historical routed session keys could be restored from persisted Control UI settings instead of being discarded back to main.

If no test was added, why not: not applicable; focused storage restore tests were added and expanded.

## Risk checklist

- User-visible behavior changed: Yes. Reloaded Control UI sessions now fall back to main when the persisted value is a historical routed session key.
- Config, environment, or migration behavior changed: No.
- Security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior changed: No.
- Highest-risk area: the key-shape classifier could accidentally treat a valid persisted session key as historical.
- Risk mitigation: the predicate is scoped to routed direct/group/dm/thread forms, and regression tests cover both sanitized historical shapes and valid non-historical agent main session keys.

## Current review state

- Next action: ClawSweeper and maintainers can re-review the refreshed branch at head 4c3425f6cf.
- Still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof: fresh ClawSweeper re-review and current-head checks on this push.

target.title: fix(feishu): preserve full bitable token and include URL in error context
target.body:
## Summary

- Loosen the bitable URL token regex so token characters beyond `[A-Za-z0-9]` (e.g. `-`, `_`) are not silently truncated.
- Include the offending URL in the `getBitableMeta` invalid-URL error so callers can debug from the thrown message alone.
- Add focused unit tests covering both the parser and the new error context.

## Root cause

`extensions/feishu/src/bitable.ts#parseBitableUrl` matched the URL token with `[A-Za-z0-9]+`, which silently truncates any token containing `-` or `_`. The current Lark token format is alphanumeric so this does not affect production today, but it makes the parser brittle to any future token format change and gives no diagnostic when an unrecognized URL is passed.

The corresponding `getBitableMeta` error message was static text (`"Invalid URL format. Expected /base/XXX or /wiki/XXX URL"`) that did not include the URL the caller passed, so the user could not tell from the thrown error which input was rejected.

## Behavior change

- `parseBitableUrl("/base/abc-123_xyz")` now returns `{ token: "abc-123_xyz", tableId: undefined, isWiki: false }` instead of being silently truncated to `{ token: "abc", ... }`.
- For invalid URLs, `getBitableMeta` now throws `Invalid bitable URL: "<url>". Expected a /base/<token> or /wiki/<token> URL.` with the original URL embedded in the message.
- `parseBitableUrl` and `getBitableMeta` are now exported (they were file-local). The extensions `tsconfig` already permits the public surface from this path, and no other module re-imports the private shape.

## Verification

- `pnpm test:extension feishu` — 570/570 tests passed (6 new tests in `extensions/feishu/src/bitable.test.ts`).
- `node_modules/.bin/oxlint extensions/feishu/src/bitable.ts extensions/feishu/src/bitable.test.ts` — 0 warnings, 0 errors.
- `node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check ...` — formatting clean.
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/feishu/src/bitable.test.ts` — 6/6 passed.

## What was not tested

- No live Lark tenant was available for a live-URL end-to-end reproduction. The new regex still rejects URLs without a `/base/<token>` or `/wiki/<token>` path segment, so the upstream `bitable.app.get` / `wiki.space.getNode` validation still receives the original token untouched. A future change that introduces a different token shape should add a test before tightening the character class.
- `extensions/feishu/src/bitable.ts` previously had no unit tests at all; this PR adds the first six. Adjacent helpers (`getAppTokenFromWiki`, `listFields`, `listRecords`, ...) are still uncovered; widening scope into them is intentionally out of scope for this narrow fix.
- This PR was committed with `FAST_COMMIT=1` because `pnpm tsgo` reports pre-existing type errors in `src/media-understanding/runner.entries.ts` and `src/process/supervisor/adapters/child.test.ts` on `origin/main` that are unrelated to this change. Local targeted verification (`pnpm test:extension feishu`, `oxfmt --check`, `oxlint`) was run manually in their place per the repo's documented `FAST_COMMIT=1` policy.

target.title: fix #70559: runUnsafeReindex crashes with "no such table: chunks_vec" when sqlite-vec is enabled
target.body:
## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?

`runUnsafeReindex` crashed with `SqliteError: no such table: chunks_vec` when sqlite-vec was enabled. Root cause: `resetIndex()` could drop the `chunks_vec` virtual table while cached vector readiness still described it as available, so later sync setup prepared vector-table deletes against a missing table.

Why does this matter now?

#70559 is a user-visible memory-core crash in unsafe reindex. Users with sqlite-vec enabled can lose the ability to rebuild the memory index cleanly, which makes memory recovery/reindex workflows unreliable.

What is the intended outcome?

Unsafe full reindex preserves the sqlite-vec table schema on the normal path, clears vector rows in place, and resets cached vector readiness if the delete path fails.

What is intentionally out of scope?

Out of scope: safe reindex, embedding provider selection, external embedding transport, config defaults, schema migrations, gateway behavior, and Windows handle-contention behavior. This PR only changes builtin memory-core unsafe reset lifecycle and its regression coverage.

What does success look like?

Unsafe reindex completes with sqlite-vec enabled, stale vector rows are removed, `chunks_vec` still exists, new indexed vector rows can be written, and vector metadata remains valid.

What should reviewers focus on?

Review focus: the unsafe-reindex vector lifecycle choice, especially whether preserving `chunks_vec` while clearing rows is the desired persisted-index reset contract.

Additional maintainer fields:

- Fix classification: root-cause fix.
- Maintainer-ready confidence: high, with the remaining decision points called out under Current review state.
- Scope boundary: one issue, two files, builtin memory-core unsafe reset lifecycle only.
- Source of truth boundary: `resetIndex()` owns the full-index reset invariant between persisted SQLite tables and cached vector readiness; downstream vector delete/insert code should not compensate for stale table-availability state.
- Related open PR scan: this is the existing contributor PR for #70559; this update does not create a competing narrower PR and treats gateway-core CI as outside the memory-core diff unless maintainers decide otherwise.

## Linked context

Closes #70559.

Related: existing PR #88696 review state and ClawSweeper RF-001 through RF-005.

This was not requested by a maintainer directly; it addresses the linked user-visible memory-core crash.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: unsafe full reindex with sqlite-vec enabled no longer crashes at the reset/prepare boundary after stale vector-table state exists.
- Real environment tested: Linux x64, Node.js 22.22.0, OpenClaw source checkout, real SQLite database, real sqlite-vec Linux x64 extension, builtin memory backend, isolated config/state/workspace, and an in-process local proof embedding provider so no external API key or network provider was required.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - Seeded an isolated SQLite memory index with real `files`, `chunks`, `meta`, and `chunks_vec` (`vec0`) tables.
  - Loaded the real sqlite-vec extension and inserted one stale vector row with `vectorDims: 4` metadata.
  - Ran the current-head memory manager sync path with `force: true` and `OPENCLAW_TEST_FAST=1` / `OPENCLAW_TEST_MEMORY_UNSAFE_REINDEX=1`, using a config that resolves to the seeded DB and vector-enabled builtin memory backend.
  - Re-opened the DB with sqlite-vec loaded and verified the virtual table still exists, stale vector/chunk/file rows are gone, new indexed rows exist, and metadata has `vectorDims: 4`.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):

  ```text
  Validation `live-sqlite-vec-proof` (pass, exit_code=0, 7590ms)
  before: sqliteVecLoadable=true chunksVecExists=true vectorRows=1 seededVectorDims=4
  resolvedConfig {"backend":"builtin","storePath":"[local path redacted]","vectorEnabled":true,"provider":"proof"}
  managerBefore ... "vector":{"enabled":true,"dims":4} ... "indexIdentity":{"status":"mismatched","reason":"index scope changed"}
  managerAfter ... "vector":{"enabled":true,"storeAvailable":true,"extensionPath":"[local path redacted]","dims":4} ... "providerState":{"mode":"active","providerId":"proof"} ... "indexIdentity":{"status":"valid"}
  after: cliExit=0 chunksVecExists=true vectorRows=1 staleVectorRows=0 chunksRows=1 staleChunkRows=0 filesRows=1 staleFileRows=0 metaWritten=true metaProvider=proof metaModel=proof-embed metaVectorDims=4
  platform: linux x64 node=22.22.0
  ```

- Observed result after fix: the unsafe reindex path completed with sqlite-vec enabled, preserved `chunks_vec`, removed the stale `seed-row`, wrote a new vector row for the indexed memory file, and persisted valid vector metadata.
- What was not tested: the original Windows live-agent handle-contention deployment was not reproduced end to end; this proof isolates the sqlite-vec reset/prepare boundary that causes the `chunks_vec` crash.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: the proof uses a local in-process proof embedding provider to avoid external API/network dependency; it validates sqlite-vec table lifecycle and vector writes, not remote embedding transport.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): source inspection and the regression test model the broken pre-fix invariant: cached vector availability can remain true while the physical `chunks_vec` table is missing, which makes later prepared deletes fail with `no such table: chunks_vec`.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-memory.config.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/index.test.ts -t 'does not prepare vector deletes after unsafe reset drops a missing vector table'` — passed, 1 test / 38 skipped.
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-memory.config.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/index.test.ts` — passed, 39 tests.
- `node <evidence>/live-sqlite-vec-proof.mjs` via `daily_fix.sh run-validation --name live-sqlite-vec-proof` — passed; real sqlite-vec table existed before and after unsafe reset, stale rows were removed, new vector rows and `vectorDims: 4` metadata were written.
- Remote `checks-node-agentic-gateway-core` failure was attributed from the CI log to `src/gateway/gateway-cli-backend.connect.test.ts > gateway cli backend connect > connects a test gateway client through the live helper`, which timed out after 5000ms in the `test/vitest/vitest.gateway-client.config.ts` shard. Local mapping for the shard was attempted with `node scripts/test-projects.mjs test/vitest/vitest.gateway-core.config.ts test/vitest/vitest.gateway-client.config.ts`; it produced repeated no-output/hang messages and was stopped without a local memory-core assertion failure. The failing test is in gateway client/live-helper code and is outside this PR's memory-core diff.

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Added/kept focused memory-core regression coverage for the stale cached-vector state: vector availability and dimensions are cached, the physical `chunks_vec` table is missing, and unsafe reindex must not prepare deletes against a missing table.

What failed before this fix, if known?

- The issue path failed with `SqliteError: no such table: chunks_vec` during unsafe reindex when sqlite-vec was enabled and vector-table lifecycle state became inconsistent.

If no test was added, why not?

- Not applicable; regression coverage and real behavior proof are both included.

## Root cause and patch quality

- Root cause: `resetIndex()` could make the physical SQLite schema and cached vector state disagree by dropping `chunks_vec` while leaving vector availability/readiness cached as usable.
- Why this is root-cause fix: the fix restores the invariant at the reset boundary itself. When sqlite-vec is available, reset clears vector rows without destroying the virtual table schema. If that path fails, cached vector dimensions/readiness/availability are cleared before later sync setup can prepare statements against stale state.
- Patch quality notes: production changes remain limited to `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-sync-ops.ts`; regression coverage is limited to `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/index.test.ts`; no public config, protocol, provider API, or schema migration is added.
- Canonical scope: `resetIndex()` is the canonical owner for unsafe full-index clearing; fixing it there avoids special-casing later vector delete preparation or hiding the problem in search/index callers.
- Target test file: `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/index.test.ts`.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes. Unsafe full reindex with sqlite-vec enabled should now complete instead of crashing when vector state was stale.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No config or environment contract changed. Yes, the persisted sqlite-vec index reset lifecycle changes for unsafe reindex: the normal path now preserves `chunks_vec` schema and clears rows in place, while fallback clears cached vector readiness before future sync work.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

What is the highest-risk area?

Persisted memory index/session-state lifecycle for sqlite-vec during unsafe reindex.

How is that risk mitigated?

- The change is limited to fixed-table memory-core reset logic.
- Normal sqlite-vec-available reset uses `DELETE FROM chunks_vec` rather than dropping the virtual table, matching the table's existing row-delete usage in vector writes.
- If vector-table delete fails, cached vector dimensions/readiness/availability are cleared so subsequent sync setup does not treat a missing table as available.
- Targeted regression, full `index.test.ts`, and live sqlite-vec proof all passed on current head.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

Contributor side: ready for maintainer review after this PR body update and submit flow. Maintainer side: decide whether to accept the unsafe-reindex sqlite-vec lifecycle contract and whether the Linux sqlite-vec boundary proof is sufficient without Windows live-agent handle-contention proof.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

- RF-001 / RF-005: `checks-node-agentic-gateway-core` was failing on the reviewed remote head. CI log attribution points to `src/gateway/gateway-cli-backend.connect.test.ts` timing out after 5000ms in the gateway-client shard, while this PR only changes memory-core reset/test files. Local shard reproduction attempt hung with no memory-core assertion output. If the check remains required, it needs CI-side resolution, rerun, waiver, or maintainer investigation.
- RF-002 / RF-003: maintainers should explicitly accept the persisted sqlite-vec lifecycle behavior: preserve `chunks_vec` on normal unsafe reset, clear rows, and reset cached vector state on fallback.
- RF-004: Windows live-agent handle-contention behavior was not reproduced end to end; current proof covers Linux sqlite-vec reset/prepare boundary with real sqlite-vec and vector writes.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

- Addressed ClawSweeper's proof concern with a current-head live sqlite-vec proof that uses the actual builtin memory backend, vector-enabled manager state, real sqlite-vec extension loading, stale vector-row seed, unsafe reindex, and post-run DB assertions.
- Addressed lifecycle-risk comments by documenting the intended reset contract and calling out the maintainer acceptance decision explicitly.
- Addressed the failing gateway-core signal by mapping it to `test/vitest/vitest.gateway-core.config.ts` plus `test/vitest/vitest.gateway-client.config.ts`, attempting local validation, and disclosing the no-output/hang limitation instead of treating it as memory-core evidence.

target.title: fix(diagnostics): populate inputMessages/outputMessages on model.call events
target.body:
## Summary

Populates `inputMessages`/`outputMessages` on model-call diagnostic events so that the `diagnostics-otel` plugin can export raw model content to OTEL backends when `captureContent` is enabled.

Related to #77391 (model-call diagnostic content capture only; toolInputs/toolOutputs remain out of scope for this PR and should stay tracked separately).

## Problem

The `DiagnosticModelCallBaseEvent` type documents `inputMessages` and `outputMessages` fields, and the OTEL plugin reads them via `assignOtelModelContentAttributes()`, but the core model-call wrapper never populated them. This meant `openclaw.content.input_messages` and `openclaw.content.output_messages` were always absent from exported OTEL spans regardless of `captureContent` configuration.

## Changes

### 1. Extract inputMessages from onPayload (not model argument)
The stream function's first argument is a provider descriptor, not the request payload. The actual messages array is only available in the `onPayload(payload, model)` callback after provider wrappers have mutated it. Input messages are now captured at this seam and stored in `ModelCallObservationState.inputMessages`.

### 2. Handle normalized stream chunks in output extraction
The runtime normalizes all provider streams to `{ type: "text_delta", delta }` before they reach the diagnostic observer. `extractTextFromChunk()` now checks normalized formats (text_delta, text) **before** raw OpenAI wire formats, so most real model streams will capture output content.

### 3. Gate content by captureContent policy before emission
Added `ContentCapturePolicy` to `ModelCallDiagnosticContext` with a `resolveContentCapturePolicy()` helper (mirrors the OTEL service's logic). `inputMessages`/`outputMessages` are only populated on diagnostic events when the corresponding policy flag is enabled. This prevents raw prompts from traveling the internal diagnostic bus when the operator has not opted in.

### 4. Tests
- All existing test fixtures updated with required `contentCapture` field.
- New test cases:
  - "respects contentCapture policy - omits inputMessages when disabled"
  - "respects contentCapture policy - omits outputMessages when disabled"
  - "captures output from normalized text_delta chunks"
- 11/11 tests passing.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed**: Diagnostic model-call events did not carry `inputMessages`/`outputMessages`, so the OTEL plugin could never export raw model content to backends like Langfuse even when `captureContent` was enabled.

**Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw gateway on macOS (Node 22), built from PR branch. OTEL exporter configured to send to Langfuse pre-production collector at `trace-pre.agent.kspmas.ksyun.com`.

**Exact steps or command run after the patch**:
1. Build PR branch: `pnpm build`
2. Replace installed OpenClaw dist with PR build
3. Restart gateway with OTEL exporter + `captureContent.enabled=true`
4. Send a test message: `openclaw agent -m "Say hello in one word" --agent main`
5. Query Langfuse API for the latest `openclaw.model.call` observation
6. Set `captureContent.enabled=false`, restart, send another message
7. Query Langfuse API again to verify content attributes are absent

**Evidence after fix**:

With `captureContent` enabled, querying Langfuse API shows content present:
```
$ python3 -c "...query Langfuse API..."
=== openclaw.model.call | trace:e5991835cfb229485ec26ce95816b4d5 | ts:2026-05-09T16:17:53.96 ===
  input_messages present: True
  input_messages (first 100): You are a personal assistant running inside OpenClaw.
  output_messages present: True
  output_messages: 哈喽！
```

With `captureContent` disabled, content attributes are absent:
```
=== openclaw.model.call | trace:b6d8900f776750c4 | ts:2026-05-09T16:10:45.54 ===
  openclaw.api = openai-completions
  openclaw.model = glm-5.1
  openclaw.model_call.request_bytes = 99725
  openclaw.model_call.response_bytes = 1600
  *** HAS CONTENT: False
```

**Observed result after fix**: The `openclaw.model.call` span now carries `openclaw.content.input_messages` and `openclaw.content.output_messages` attributes when `captureContent` is enabled, and omits them when disabled. The policy gate works end-to-end through the core wrapper, diagnostic event bus, and OTEL exporter.

**What was not tested**: Swift/macOS app build (unrelated to this change); OTEL backends other than Langfuse.

- Validate it as a string array, trim entries, drop blank/whitespace-only entries, preserve explicit `[]`, and reject unsupported ACP paths instead of silently ignoring the policy.
- Forward and persist the allowlist through Gateway/session/embedded-attempt surfaces so later native child turns inherit the stored tool policy.
- Normalize Gateway `agent.run` `toolsAllow` before dispatching to the runner, matching the `sessions_spawn` normalization behavior.
- Keep CLI-backed runtime handling aligned with current `main`: runtime `toolsAllow` is forwarded to the CLI harness so the harness can fail closed when it cannot enforce the allowlist.

## Current upstream status
Latest `upstream/main` already includes broad subagent/runtime work and embedded-runner `toolsAllow` support. This PR remains scoped to the missing public/control-plane plumbing for `sessions_spawn.toolsAllow`, plus Gateway normalization for raw `agent.run` inputs.

## Change Type

## Scope
- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] Documentation

## Linked Issue/PR
- Related #78381
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `sessions_spawn` callers can constrain native subagent tools with `toolsAllow`; the same policy is persisted on child sessions and inherited by later native child turns when no explicit override is passed. Gateway `agent.run` also normalizes direct `toolsAllow` input before runner dispatch.

Real environment tested: macOS 26.2, Node v25.9.0, pnpm 11.2.2, local OpenClaw source checkout at PR head `8ffac4d1fc59032d23f7a97a2bf1a03752fbb3e6`, temporary worktree `/Users/guanbear/.config/superpowers/worktrees/OctoClaw/resolve-pr-78441`, and local terminal execution of OpenClaw's runtime/test commands against the merged PR code.

target.title: perf(memory): coalesce + cache session-file listings to cut NFS READDIR load
target.body:
## Summary

Repeated session-directory scans (`listSessionFilesForAgent`) issue a `fs.readdir(dir, { withFileTypes: true })` per call with **zero caching or coalescing** across call sites. On networked filesystems (NFS) this is expensive: resolving each entry's type turns one logical scan into roughly one attribute fetch per session file, and several subsystems (startup catch-up, incremental sync, dreaming, qmd export) scan the same agent sessions dir in close succession — producing redundant READDIR/GETATTR load.

This adds an in-flight dedupe + short-TTL snapshot cache around `listSessionFilesForAgent`:

- **Coalescing** — concurrent callers join a single in-flight `Promise`, so a burst of scans triggers one READDIR instead of N.
- **Short-TTL snapshot** — a resolved listing is reused briefly between scans. The cache is keyed by the **resolved sessions directory** (folds in `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, and lines up with `path.dirname(update.sessionFile)` for precise invalidation).
- **Event-driven invalidation (the correctness path)** — a single lazy subscription to the canonical `onSessionTranscriptUpdate` event drops the affected directory's snapshot. Every in-process mutation to a session-owned path (append, compaction, rewrite, chat inject, command exec) emits this event, and archive rotation (`archiveFileOnDisk`) already emits through it too, so one canonical hook covers all in-process write paths without scattered per-path invalidation.
- **TTL as a backstop only** — `SESSION_FILES_LISTING_TTL_MS = 5_000` bounds staleness for writers that never reach our event bus (e.g. another node sharing the NFS sessions dir, or low-frequency archive prunes).
- **Failed scans are never cached** — read results are a discriminated union (`{ ok: true; files } | { ok: false }`). A transient `fs.readdir` failure (NFS `EIO`/`ESTALE`/`EACCES`/timeout) is returned to the one caller as empty but **not published** into the snapshot, so the next call retries instead of serving a false-empty session set for the TTL window. A legitimately absent dir (`ENOENT`, the normal "no sessions yet" state) is still cached as empty, since the first session write emits an invalidation event.

The cache is bounded (64 dirs), module-lifecycle-owned, and race-safe: a completed read only publishes its snapshot if its entry is still the active one, so an invalidation or newer read during the await wins. A `resetSessionFilesListingCache()` helper exists for test isolation and is intentionally kept out of the package's public re-export surface.

`withFileTypes` removal (to cut the per-entry NFS `lstat` amplification) is deferred to a focused follow-up.

## Verification

### Committed regression tests

`node scripts/run-vitest.mjs packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/session-files.test.ts` — **18/18** (12 pre-existing + 6 new). The cache tests count real scans by spying on `fsPromises.readdir`:

- **concurrent coalescing** — two un-awaited concurrent calls ⇒ `readdir` called **exactly once**.
- **cached-within-TTL** — a second call after writing a new file *without* emitting an event still returns the prior snapshot ⇒ **1** scan.
- **refresh-after-TTL** — with `vi.useFakeTimers({ toFake: ["Date"] })`, advancing `Date` past 5s forces a re-scan ⇒ **2** scans, fresh listing.
- **event invalidation** — `emitSessionTranscriptUpdate({ sessionFile })` drops the snapshot ⇒ next call re-scans, fresh listing.
- **failed-scan not cached** *(new, addresses review)* — `readdir` rejected once with `EIO` ⇒ first call returns `[]`, a second call within the TTL **re-scans** (2 scans) and recovers the real listing.
- **absent-dir cached** *(new)* — a missing sessions dir (`ENOENT`) is cached as empty ⇒ **1** scan across two calls.

Other proof:

- Consumer suites green: `manager-sync-ops.startup-catchup`, `manager-targeted-sync`, `manager-session-sync-state`, `qmd-manager`, `manager.fts-only-reindex`, `manager-source-state`, `dreaming-phases`, `manager-sync-yield`, `session-files-yield`.
- Typecheck: `pnpm tsgo:core` and `pnpm tsgo:test:packages` exit 0. `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs` clean; `oxfmt --check` clean.

### Concurrency chaos / fuzz hammering (dev-time stress; not committed)

A seeded invariant-based harness drove **randomized interleavings** of concurrent read bursts + real file create/delete + `onSessionTranscriptUpdate` events against the live cache, asserting:

- **coalescing by reference identity** — every caller in a synchronous burst receives the *same array instance* (`===`), proving they joined one read rather than each scanning;
- **no phantom files** — every returned name is a real, valid session file that actually existed;
- **eventual consistency** — after a final invalidation, the listing equals on-disk ground truth exactly;
- **cache-bound correctness** — with agent count > the 64-dir bound, evicted entries re-read fresh (never stale/wrong).

Ran at **20,000 iterations × burst 32 × 200–300 agents across 5 seeds** — all green. (Kept as a local stress tool rather than a committed suite to avoid a long-running test in CI; the committed unit tests above are the durable regression guard.)

### Mutation testing (dev-time; confirms the tests actually catch breakage)

Each cache invariant was corrupted one at a time in `session-files.ts`; the unit + chaos suites were re-run to confirm they **kill** the mutant, then the source was restored (verified clean via `git diff`):

| Mutant | Killed by | Result |
|---|---|---|
| re-cache failed scan (the bug this PR's fix removes) | unit | ✅ killed |
| drop event-driven invalidation | unit + chaos | ✅ killed |
| TTL zero (cache disabled) | unit | ✅ killed |
| disable TTL cache read (never serve snapshot) | unit | ✅ killed |
| no coalesce-join (no shared in-flight) | unit + chaos | ✅ killed |
| treat ENOENT as failure (stop caching empty dirs) | unit | ✅ killed |
| drop race-guard on publish | — | ⚪ survives (expected) |
| max-dirs bound 64 → 100000 | — | ⚪ survives (perf-only) |

**6/6 correctness mutants killed, 0 unexpected survivors.** The surviving `drop-race-guard` is expected: reads *replace* the map entry rather than mutating it, so a stale completion writing into an orphaned entry is harmless — the guard is documented defensive intent, not load-bearing. `max-dirs` is a perf knob, not a correctness property.

target.title: fix(memory-core): write ## Deep Sleep summary to DREAMS.md
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: The Deep Sleep phase writes per-phase report files (`memory/dreaming/deep/YYYY-MM-DD.md`) but never writes the documented `## Deep Sleep` summary section to `DREAMS.md`. Per docs: "Deep phase writes a `## Deep Sleep` summary into `DREAMS.md` and optionally writes `memory/dreaming/deep/YYYY-MM-DD.md`."
- What changed: `writeDeepDreamingReport` now always writes the `## Deep Sleep` summary to `DREAMS.md`. When an existing `## Deep Sleep` section is present, it is replaced in-place (including when the heading appears at file position 0 without a leading newline). Other sections in DREAMS.md are preserved. The separate per-phase report file is still conditionally written when storage mode requests it. Added test coverage for separateReports=false (DREAMS.md only) and section replacement (non-destructive, no duplicates).

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix

## Linked Issues

- Closes #91051

## Real behavior proof

target.body:
## Summary
- Add optional `toolsAllow` to `sessions_spawn` for native subagent runs.
- Validate it as a string array, trim entries, drop blank/whitespace-only entries, preserve explicit `[]`, and reject unsupported ACP paths instead of silently ignoring the policy.
- Forward and persist the allowlist through Gateway/session/embedded-attempt surfaces so later native child turns inherit the stored tool policy.
- Normalize Gateway `agent.run` `toolsAllow` before dispatching to the runner, matching the `sessions_spawn` normalization behavior.
- Keep CLI-backed runtime handling aligned with current `main`: runtime `toolsAllow` is forwarded to the CLI harness so the harness can fail closed when it cannot enforce the allowlist.

## Current upstream status
Latest `upstream/main` already includes broad subagent/runtime work and embedded-runner `toolsAllow` support. This PR remains scoped to the missing public/control-plane plumbing for `sessions_spawn.toolsAllow`, plus Gateway normalization for raw `agent.run` inputs.

## Change Type
- [x] Feature

## Scope
- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] Documentation

## Linked Issue/PR
- Related #78381
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `sessions_spawn` callers can constrain native subagent tools with `toolsAllow`; the same policy is persisted on child sessions and inherited by later native child turns when no explicit override is passed. Gateway `agent.run` also normalizes direct `toolsAllow` input before runner dispatch.

Real environment tested: macOS 26.2, Node v25.9.0, pnpm 11.2.2, local OpenClaw source checkout at PR head `8ffac4d1fc59032d23f7a97a2bf1a03752fbb3e6`, temporary worktree `/Users/guanbear/.config/superpowers/worktrees/OctoClaw/resolve-pr-78441`, and local terminal execution of OpenClaw's runtime/test commands against the merged PR code.

